title: Your First Claude API Call
description: Authenticate, make calls, handle responses. From zero to your first working AI integration.
learningObjectives: I can make a successful API call to Claude with the Anthropic SDK
I can understand the request and response structure
I can handle the API key securely

contentSections.body: Alex's team was waiting 3 days for a vendor to integrate AI into their internal tool. He built the same integration in 90 minutes using the Claude API. That's the power of direct API access — no middleman, full control.
**Never hardcode your API key in source code.** Anyone with access to your code (GitHub, co-workers, deployment logs) can see it and rack up charges on your account. Always use environment variables.

contentSections.question: Why do you access `response.content[0].text` instead of just `response.text`?
contentSections.answer: The API returns an array of content blocks — because responses can include multiple types (text, images, tool calls). `content[0]` gets the first block, and `.text` gets the text from a text block. Always check `content[0].type === 'text'` in production code.
contentSections.hint: The response is designed to handle multiple content types — why might there be an array?

contentSections.body: API key in .env, never in code. Response text lives at `response.content[0].text`.

contentSections.question: When would you choose Haiku over Sonnet for an API call?
contentSections.answer: For high-volume, simple tasks: classifying short texts, extracting structured data, answering FAQ questions, basic summarization. Haiku is 10× cheaper — for 10,000 API calls/day, that's the difference between $50 and $500. Use Sonnet for complex reasoning, code generation, nuanced writing.
contentSections.hint: Think about cost and task complexity — when does 'good enough but cheap' beat 'best and expensive'?

contentSections.body: Haiku for volume/speed. Sonnet for quality. Opus for maximum capability. Always set max_tokens explicitly.

title: Error Handling & Rate Limits
description: What happens when API calls fail. Rate limits, retries, timeouts — build robust AI integrations.
learningObjectives: I can handle the three most common Claude API errors
I can implement exponential backoff for retries
I can explain what rate limits are and how to work within them

contentSections.body: Sarah shipped her AI-powered tool. It worked great in testing. Then 100 users tried to use it simultaneously. Half got blank screens — no error messages, no fallbacks. Her app had no error handling. Real apps handle failures. Always.

contentSections.question: Why should you always wrap API calls in try/catch?
contentSections.answer: API calls can fail for many reasons: network issues, rate limits, server errors, invalid inputs. Without try/catch, an unhandled error crashes your entire app. With it, you can show the user a helpful message, retry automatically, or fall back to a default behavior.
contentSections.hint: What happens to your app if an API call throws and nothing catches it?

contentSections.body: Always wrap API calls in try/catch. Handle 401 (auth), 429 (rate limit), and 5xx (server errors) differently.

**The Anthropic SDK handles retries automatically.** Pass `maxRetries` when initializing: `new Anthropic({ maxRetries: 3 })`. For most apps, let the SDK handle it. Roll your own only for custom retry logic.

contentSections.question: Why use exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) instead of retrying immediately or with constant delays?
contentSections.answer: Immediate retries amplify the problem — if you hit a rate limit and 100 clients retry at once, you hit it again instantly. Exponential backoff spreads retries over time, giving the server room to recover. Constant delays waste time. Exponential is the right tradeoff.
contentSections.hint: Think about what happens if 100 clients all hit a rate limit and retry at the same instant

contentSections.body: Exponential backoff: retry with increasing waits. The SDK handles this — configure maxRetries on initialization.

contentSections.title: Rate limits and how to stay within them

title: Streaming — Real-Time AI Responses
description: Stream tokens as they generate instead of waiting for the full response. Makes AI feel instant.
learningObjectives: I can implement streaming with the Claude API
I can explain why streaming improves user experience
I can handle streaming errors gracefully

contentSections.body: Without streaming: the user waits 5 seconds staring at a spinner, then sees the full response appear at once. With streaming: text appears word by word, starting in under a second. Same response time — completely different feel. Streaming is the difference between a chatbot and a ghost.

contentSections.question: A user submits a question and the response takes 6 seconds to generate. With streaming, when does the user start seeing text?
contentSections.answer: Within milliseconds of the first token being generated — typically under 500ms. The user sees text appearing continuously for 6 seconds. Without streaming, they see nothing for 6 seconds, then everything at once. Same total time, completely different perceived experience.
contentSections.hint: Streaming sends each token as it's generated — when is the first token ready?

contentSections.body: Streaming starts showing text in milliseconds. Use `.messages.stream()` instead of `.messages.create()` for any user-facing feature.
**Server-Sent Events (SSE)** is the standard protocol for streaming from server to browser. The `text/event-stream` content type tells the browser to expect a stream. Each `data:` line is one event.

contentSections.question: Why do you need a backend server to proxy Claude API calls — why not call Claude directly from the browser?
contentSections.answer: Your API key would be exposed in browser source code — anyone could see it and use it (costing you money). A backend proxy keeps the key secret on the server, validates user authentication before calling Claude, and lets you add rate limiting, caching, and logging.
contentSections.hint: Think about what anyone with browser devtools could see in your frontend code

contentSections.body: Never expose API keys in frontend code. Always proxy through a backend. Use SSE format for streaming to browsers.
